---
title: "Decisions"
pagetitle: "Skein Lite: Decisions"
execute:
  freeze: true
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import json
S = json.load(open("../evaluation/reports/site_stats.json"))
R = S["routing"]
AB = json.load(open("../evaluation/reports/gpt_oss_swap_ab.json"))
F = AB["overall"]["faithfulness"]
def pct(x): return f"{x:g}%"
```

::: {.eyebrow}
Kept and rejected, on evidence
:::

Sara is one deterministic orchestrator over one gated pipeline, Groq-only. Each of those was a decision with a live alternative, and several alternatives were built, measured, and removed. The scorecard first, the reasoning after.

## The scorecard

| Decision | Kept | Rejected | Decided by |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control flow (default) | one deterministic orchestrator (`rag/router.py`) | a LangGraph brain on the fast path, benchmarked and moved off it | routing eval, plus one safety surface instead of two |
| Hard-tail lane (opt-in) | a LangGraph corrective-RAG lane, off the default (`CHAT_BRAIN=graph`) | putting it on the fast path | it helps a narrow tail at higher cost, so it is available, not default |
| Serving path | one gated pipeline (`pipeline/answer.py`) | per-lane pipelines | every turn passes the same guards |
| Release | human-gated promotion, everywhere | any auto-promote path | the reward-hacking guardrail |
| Workhorse model | `llama-3.3-70b` | a `gpt-oss-120b` swap | faithfulness `{python} F["baseline"]` vs `{python} F["candidate"]` on the graded golden set |
| Routing tie-break | the 8B small model | a 70B tie-break | `{python} pct(R["tiebreak_8b_pct"])` vs `{python} pct(R["tiebreak_70b_pct"])`, the big model loses |
| Prompt tuning | an ~60-line OPRO loop | DSPy | +`{python} S["prompt_opt"]["delta_pts"]` points held-out, no framework adopted |
| Hallucination check | an offline sampled judge | an in-loop critic before streaming | the answer streams; the judge never blocks it |

## One orchestrator, no framework brain

Every turn goes through a cheap-first cascade: a deterministic escalation intercept, deterministic intent guards, then one 8B tie-break call only for genuine ambiguity. The lane then streams through the single gated pipeline, so the stylist, care, complaint, and escalation lanes all inherit the same grounding and safety rules.

A LangGraph supervisor brain once ran every turn as a state machine. It was benchmarked and moved off the default path: two brains on the fast path meant two safety surfaces to keep in parity, and the deterministic cascade won on the routing eval. That is the decision, and it still stands for the default turn.

LangGraph did not leave the repo, though, because retiring a tool from the fast path is not the same as it being the wrong tool everywhere. It returns scoped and opt-in as a corrective/self-RAG lane (`CHAT_BRAIN=graph`, `rag/graph_brain.py`) for the hard tail: a `retrieve → grade → rewrite and re-retrieve → generate → grade → reflect or abstain` loop, bounded by a retry counter, the same per-turn `TurnBudget`, and a recursion backstop. The reason it is opt-in and not default is the same reason the gpt-oss swap was held and the 8B tie-break beat the 70B: it earns its keep only on a narrow, retrieval-heavy tail, at higher latency and cost. Crucially, every answer it produces still runs through the one gated pipeline (`stream_answer`), so the order-PII, injection, enumeration, and grounding gates are unchanged and there is still exactly one safety surface. The graph adds a retrieval strategy, never a second gate. A `linear` no-routing mode also remains behind the flag; the old `agent` value maps to the orchestrator.

## No auto-promote, anywhere

Drift detection, the CT cycle, the prompt loop, consolidation, and staleness all follow one rule: propose automatically, promote through a human. A system that rewrites its own served behavior invites drift and reward hacking, so the loop stops at a registered `@challenger` and shadow evidence, and a person runs the promotion command. The whole loop is on the [MLOps](mlops.qmd) page.

## The model swap that was measured and held

`gpt-oss-120b` is cheaper per token than the 70B workhorse and looked like a free win: the aggregate RAGAS delta on the `{python} S["golden_items"]`-item graded golden set was `{python} AB["aggregate"]["delta"]`. But faithfulness fell from `{python} F["baseline"]` to `{python} F["candidate"]`, and faithfulness is the metric a shopper feels as a made-up fact. The swap was held. Saving money by inventing more is not saving money.

## The 8B tie-break over a 70B, and Groq-only

On the `{python} S["routing_eval_set"]`-turn routing eval, the 70B tie-break scored `{python} pct(R["tiebreak_70b_pct"])` against the 8B's `{python} pct(R["tiebreak_8b_pct"])`, so a model many times the price lost the classification job. The 8B ships, with `{python} pct(R["tiebreak_8b_escalation_recall_pct"])` escalation recall, and the system stays on one vendor. A bigger model is reserved for the one place it earns its cost: an offline judge, never the serving path.

## An OPRO loop over DSPy

The prompts are hand-tuned and safety-critical, so adopting a framework that wants to own the prompting layer was a large dependency for a small gain. The loop in `mlops/prompt_opt.py` is about sixty lines: an LLM proposes rewrites from the failing cases, the eval scores them on a held-out split, a safety gate checks the contract, and a human promotes the winner. It lifted the tie-break prompt from `{python} pct(S["prompt_opt"]["baseline_pct"])` to `{python} pct(S["prompt_opt"]["candidate_pct"])` held-out, which is the gain that mattered, without the framework.

## An offline judge over an in-loop critic

A critic model that reviews every answer before the shopper sees it doubles latency and cost on every turn. Instead the answer streams immediately, and the faithfulness ladder queues low-grounding turns plus a sampled slice for a different-family judge after the answer is sent. Hallucinations are caught on a cadence that costs a fraction of a critic, and streaming is never blocked. Details on the [Evaluation](evaluation.qmd#hallucination-defense-three-layers) page.

## The stack, briefly

| Component | Chosen | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Embeddings + rerank | Cohere `embed-v4.0`, `rerank-v3.5` | the reranker is the highest-leverage retrieval component; one vendor, one seam, strong in English and French |
| LLM serving | Groq | tokens per second is a feature in a streaming chat; open-weight model, no lock-in |
| Vectors | Qdrant hybrid | dense + lexical in one store, local-first |
| Numbers | a governed metric layer (`metrics.yaml`) over read-only DuckDB | "what's the cheapest hoodie in stock" runs a parameterized SQL template; the model presents the number and never invents it |
| Relationships | Neo4j, scoped | used only where a graph hop beats similarity search; one of two modeled relationships is thin, and that is documented, not hidden |
| Lakehouse | DuckDB + dbt medallion | tested, reproducible governance that runs on a laptop and in CI |

Everything sits behind a factory seam (`make_llm`, `make_embedder`, `make_reranker`), so each row is a config change, not a rewrite. The project already survived one migration this way, Voyage to Cohere.

## Alternatives at a glance

| Component | Chosen | Alternative | Switch when |
|---|---|---|---|
| Embeddings | Cohere `embed-v4.0` | sentence-transformers (local, free) or OpenAI | you want zero quota, or you are already on OpenAI |
| Reranker | Cohere `rerank-v3.5` | a local cross-encoder | you need fully offline, or lower cost at volume |
| LLM | Groq Llama 3.3 70B | a frontier model | you need deeper multi-step reasoning over streaming speed |
| Vectors | Qdrant hybrid | pgvector, Weaviate | you want vectors inside your existing Postgres |
| Lakehouse | DuckDB + dbt | Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks | data outgrows a laptop, or a team needs a shared warehouse |
| Graph | Neo4j | a relational adjacency model | the graph stays small enough for SQL |

## If the stakes change

- **Regulated domain.** The masking tests, read-only governed queries, and the deterministic order-PII gate are the spine; add an audit log on disclosure, retention on traces, and mandatory human review in the existing escalation queue.
- **Multi-tenant B2B.** A domain is already a config folder, so a tenant is a pack plus an isolated index and graph; order access binds to the authenticated principal.
- **A shared warehouse.** The dbt project ports as-is; DuckDB stays as the fast local build for CI.
- **More traffic.** Redis behind the rate-limit interface, managed tiers for the stores, and the shadow step grows into a live canary once the loop has earned it.
